title: Volta builds the first battery (1800)
what: Alessandro Volta stacked discs of zinc and copper separated by brine-soaked cloth, producing the first steady source of electric current.
why: Before the 'voltaic pile', electricity was only fleeting static sparks; Volta gave science a continuous current to experiment with.
care: Every battery in your phone, car, and remote control is a descendant of this simple stack of metal discs.

title: Faraday discovers electromagnetic induction (1831)
what: Michael Faraday showed that moving a magnet through a coil of wire generates an electric current.
why: Induction is the principle behind every generator and transformer — turning motion into electricity and changing its voltage.
care: Almost all the electricity you use, from any source, is made by spinning magnets past coils exactly as Faraday described.

title: Edison's Pearl Street Station opens (1882)
what: In September 1882 Edison's Pearl Street plant in Manhattan began selling electricity to paying customers, lighting a few hundred lamps.
why: It was the first commercial central power station — proof that electricity could be generated centrally and sold as a utility.
care: It created the business model — a power company that bills you monthly — that still powers your home today.

title: The first hydroelectric plant (1882)
what: Weeks after Pearl Street, the Vulcan Street plant in Appleton, Wisconsin used the Fox River to generate the world's first hydroelectric power.
why: It showed falling water could replace burning coal to make electricity — the first renewable power station.
care: Hydropower still supplies about a sixth of the world's electricity, all tracing back to this small mill-town experiment.

title: Tesla patents the AC induction motor (1888)
what: Nikola Tesla patented a practical motor that runs on alternating current using a rotating magnetic field.
why: It gave alternating current a killer application — a rugged, brushless motor — and helped AC win out over Edison's DC.
care: The same induction motor runs your fridge, washing machine, fans, and most electric-vehicle drivetrains.

title: Niagara Falls powers Buffalo by AC (1896)
what: Westinghouse's Niagara Falls plant sent alternating current 26 miles to Buffalo, New York.
why: It proved AC could move large amounts of power over long distances — the decisive win in the 'war of the currents'.
care: Long-distance transmission is why a power plant can sit far from your city and still keep your lights on.

title: The Rural Electrification Act (1936)
what: A U.S. law funded cooperatives to string power lines to farms and villages that private utilities had ignored as unprofitable.
why: In 1935 only about 1 in 10 American farms had electricity; within a decade most did.
care: It shows that universal electricity often needs public effort, not just markets — a debate still live in the developing world.

title: Hoover Dam begins generating (1936)
what: The 221-metre Hoover Dam on the Colorado River started producing hydroelectricity during the Great Depression.
why: It was a landmark of large-scale public infrastructure, supplying power and water across the U.S. Southwest.
care: Mega-dams like it still anchor the grids and water supplies of entire regions — and spark debate over their environmental cost.

title: Britain's National Grid switches on (1935)
what: The UK linked regional power stations into a single synchronised national network, completed around 1935.
why: Pooling stations let the country share electricity, balance demand, and back each other up during failures.
care: It pioneered the idea of a nationwide grid — the model nearly every country now relies on.

title: The first grid-connected nuclear plant (1954)
what: The Obninsk station in the USSR became the first nuclear reactor to deliver electricity to a public grid — about 5 megawatts.
why: It proved fission could power cities, not just weapons, opening the civilian nuclear age.
care: Every nuclear kilowatt-hour you use today descends from this small proof-of-concept.

title: Calder Hall, first industrial-scale nuclear (1956)
what: Britain's Calder Hall became the world's first nuclear power station to deliver electricity on an industrial scale.
why: It moved nuclear power from laboratory demonstration to genuine commercial output.
care: It began the fleet of large reactors that today supply roughly a tenth of the world's electricity.

title: Shippingport, first U.S. commercial reactor (1957)
what: Shippingport in Pennsylvania became the first full-scale nuclear plant built solely to make civilian electricity in the U.S.
why: It launched America's commercial nuclear programme and tested reactor designs that influenced later plants.
care: It set the U.S. on a path where nuclear still provides about half of the country's carbon-free electricity.

title: Bell Labs unveils the silicon solar cell (1954)
what: Bell Labs demonstrated the first practical silicon photovoltaic cell, converting about 6% of sunlight into electricity.
why: It was the first solar cell efficient enough to be useful, first powering satellites and remote equipment.
care: That same silicon technology, now far cheaper and more efficient, sits on rooftops and solar farms worldwide.

title: The Tennessee Valley Authority is created (1933)
what: A U.S. federal agency built dams and power plants across the Tennessee Valley to provide electricity, flood control, and jobs.
why: It electrified one of America's poorest regions and became a model of integrated regional development.
care: Public power authorities born from this era still run major parts of the grid and keep rates affordable for millions.

title: The Northeast Blackout of 1965
what: A single mis-set protective relay in Ontario cascaded into a blackout across the U.S. Northeast, darkening ~30 million people for up to 13 hours.
why: It exposed how a tiny fault could topple an interconnected grid like dominoes.
care: The reliability rules and coordinating bodies (now NERC) that keep your lights on during peak demand were created in response.

title: The Three Mile Island accident (1979)
what: A partial meltdown at a Pennsylvania reactor released a small amount of radiation after equipment failures and operator confusion.
why: Though no one was hurt, it badly shook public trust and effectively froze new U.S. reactor orders for decades.
care: It still shapes how nuclear plants are regulated, staffed, and perceived in the United States today.

title: The Chernobyl disaster (1986)
what: A flawed reactor design and unsafe test caused an explosion and fire at the Chernobyl plant in Soviet Ukraine, spreading radiation across Europe.
why: It was the worst nuclear accident in history and forced a global rethink of reactor safety and openness.
care: Modern reactors are designed specifically to make a Chernobyl-style runaway physically impossible.

title: The 2003 Northeast Blackout
what: Overgrown trees, a software bug, and an alarm failure cascaded into a blackout across the U.S. Northeast and Ontario, hitting ~50 million people.
why: It showed that even a modern grid can fall to a chain of small, unnoticed faults.
care: It drove mandatory reliability standards and tree-trimming rules that protect today's grid from repeat cascades.
src: U.S.-Canada Power System Outage Task Force

title: The Fukushima Daiichi accident (2011)
what: A magnitude-9 earthquake and tsunami knocked out cooling at Japan's Fukushima Daiichi plant, causing three reactor meltdowns.
why: It triggered mass evacuations and led several countries to rethink or phase out nuclear power.
care: It reshaped global energy policy — Germany's nuclear exit and Japan's pivot both trace to this day.

title: The first wind farm (1980)
what: The world's first wind farm — 20 turbines — was installed on Crotched Mountain in New Hampshire in 1980.
why: It tested whether clusters of turbines could feed meaningful power to a grid, despite early reliability problems.
care: Wind now generates more electricity than any other renewable in many countries, all starting from experiments like this.

title: The first offshore wind farm (1991)
what: Denmark built Vindeby, the world's first offshore wind farm, with 11 turbines in shallow Baltic waters.
why: It proved turbines could survive at sea, where winds are stronger and steadier than on land.
care: Offshore wind is now a backbone of Europe's clean-energy plans and is expanding off U.S. and Asian coasts.

title: Sony commercialises the lithium-ion battery (1991)
what: Sony released the first commercial lithium-ion rechargeable battery, far lighter and denser than earlier types.
why: It made portable electronics genuinely practical and, decades later, made electric cars and grid storage viable.
care: The battery in your phone and the packs in electric cars and home power-walls are all lithium-ion.

title: Three Gorges Dam reaches full power (2012)
what: China's Three Gorges Dam on the Yangtze became fully operational with 22,500 megawatts — the largest power station on Earth.
why: It demonstrated hydropower at a scale no other project has matched, for both electricity and flood control.
care: A single structure now produces as much power as roughly 20 large nuclear reactors — at a major environmental and social cost.

title: Itaipu Dam begins operating (1984)
what: The Itaipu Dam on the Paraná River, shared by Brazil and Paraguay, started generating; for years it led the world in annual output.
why: It is a rare bi-national power plant, supplying a huge share of two countries' electricity.
care: Itaipu still provides most of Paraguay's power and a large slice of Brazil's — a model of shared energy infrastructure.

title: The 1973 oil crisis
what: An OPEC oil embargo quadrupled crude prices, causing fuel shortages and long queues across the industrialised world.
why: It revealed how dependent modern economies were on imported oil and sparked the first serious push for efficiency and alternatives.
care: Today's interest in energy independence, efficiency standards, and renewables was largely born in this crisis.

title: Ohm publishes his law (1827)
what: Georg Ohm showed that current equals voltage divided by resistance — a simple, reliable relationship in a circuit.
why: It turned electricity from a curiosity into something engineers could calculate and design with.
care: Ohm's law underlies the rating of every wire, fuse, and appliance that keeps your home safe.

title: Edison's practical light bulb (1879)
what: Edison demonstrated a carbon-filament bulb that glowed for many hours — the first commercially practical incandescent lamp.
why: A long-lasting, affordable bulb created the demand that justified building electricity networks at all.
care: Electric lighting was the first reason ordinary homes wanted power — the spark for the whole electrified world.

title: Godalming, first public electricity supply (1881)
what: The English town of Godalming ran the world's first public electricity supply, using a waterwheel and dynamo to light streets and homes.
why: It was an early test of electricity as a public service rather than a private novelty.
care: Municipal power — your town or city running the grid — began with experiments exactly like this.

title: Grand Coulee Dam starts up (1942)
what: The Grand Coulee Dam on the Columbia River became the largest U.S. power producer, its output ramping up during World War II.
why: Its cheap, abundant power fuelled wartime aluminium and aircraft production in the Pacific Northwest.
care: It shows how electricity supply can decide where industries — and jobs — are built.

title: Nations sign the ITER fusion agreement (2006)
what: Seven partners covering more than half the world's population agreed to jointly build ITER, a giant experimental fusion reactor in France.
why: It is one of the largest scientific collaborations ever, aiming to prove fusion can produce net energy.
care: If fusion succeeds, it promises near-limitless clean power — and this treaty is the project trying to get there.
